(1 comment)

Patch Set 4:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 4:

I'd consider another approach where LASSERT() is turned into CERROR() if it's a release.
given this isn't a frequent issue (but still important), LBUG() would be a good way to bring attention to the issue.

Patch Set 4:

Code-wise looks good to me.

Patch Set 4:

Alex,

So, a compiler directive that checks the version for "Y" < 50 in "X.Y" version, and CERROR, if Y >= 50, LBUG?

Hm.  I like the idea.  Andreas, any thoughts?

Patch Set 4: Code-Review-1

(3 comments)

> So, a compiler directive that checks the version for "Y" < 50 in
 > "X.Y" version, and CERROR, if Y >= 50, LBUG?
 > 
 > Hm.  I like the idea.  Andreas, any thoughts?

We could turn LINVRNT() into that?  It is intended for "expensive" checks, but could be enabled for development builds, unless the performance impact is too high (I don't know as I've never used it).

Patch Set 4:

(1 comment)

"We could turn LINVRNT() into that?  It is intended for "expensive" checks, but could be enabled for development builds, unless the performance impact is too high (I don't know as I've never used it)."

I'm not sure about the performance impact.  A few of them are obviously expensive (full walks of lists, etc), but most are fine.  Some of the expensive ones are probably expensive enough to screw up perf testing of dev builds.

At least the last few times Cray tried to use it, it was badly stale and crashed.  (We did push a few patches fixing things, but I don't think we ever got it fully working.)

Seems like significant cleanup work on its own.

parents.subject: LU-13194 tests: check server version sanityn 104

commit.subject: LU-11939 tgt: Do not assert during grant cleanup
commit.message:
LU-11939 tgt: Do not assert during grant cleanup

Client/server grant inconsistencies discovered during
cleanup are indicative of a bug, but any problems they
would cause have already occurred at this point.

So do not assert during this cleanup.
